title: 2025/02/13 - FD [Part 1]
displayTitle: 2025-02-13 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - International treaty examination of the Agreement Under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea on the Conservation and Sustainable Use... [Part 1]
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: International treaty examination of the Agreement Under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea on the Conservation and Sustainable Use...
description: Thursday 13 February 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee International treaty examination of the Agreement Under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea on the Conservation and Sustainable Use of Marine Biological Diversity of Areas Beyond National Jurisdiction; International treaty examination of the Agreement on Climate Change, Trade and Sustainability
descriptionSummary: International treaty examination of the Agreement Under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea on the Conservation and Sustainable Use.... International treaty examination of the Agreement Under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea on the Conservation and Sustainable Use...

chunks.text: Morning, Joanna. Yes. Excellent. Welcome along to our committee today. Thank you. Wonderful to have you with us. Um, we are live streaming this, this is the, uh, hearing session for the BBMJ in terms of the biodiversity beyond national jurisdiction. Uh, we have seen your written submission.

We welcome you to speak to that. Uh, we have 10 minutes allocated and I'm sure there'll be some questions from members as well. But over to you. Thank You. Wonderful ko. Um, I'm a professor of international law at Victoria, university of Wellington to aka I was a specialty in international law, and as my submission mentions, I was, um, privileged to be a me, a member of the New Zealand delegation to the negotiations of this treaty.

Um, I've written extensively about the treaty and its negotiations and spoken to international audiences about it. Um, so my written submissions explain why I strongly support New Zealand ratifying the BB and j agreement, and I'm happy to answer any questions that might arise from that. But perhaps before we do, I might just respond to a few of the questions that were asked in the hearing a couple of weeks ago.

Um, so first of all, just for some context, of course, the 1982 United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea or unclos, created the framework for the modern maritime zones that we have today. And of course, New Zealand is a, a big beneficiary of those zones. Um, however, the areas beyond national jurisdiction, the high seas, um, are largely governed by freedoms of the high seas, and that makes it very difficult to regulate activities, um, which are increasing on the high seas.

Um, while there are organizations that manage different sectoral activities such as fishing and mining and shipping, um, these are very much individual and there's not been any mechanism to coordinate, um, those activities or to deal with new and emerging activities or to establish marine protected areas on the high seas.

We are also seeing that the environmental impact assessment obligations have not been adequately implemented by states. So the bb and j agreement really provides an opportunity to fill the gaps in, in those contexts. Questions in the previous session, um, arose around the, the intersection between the BB and j agreement and existing organizations.

So, um, I thought I'd just talk about that briefly. Um, the, one of the principles underlying the negotiations was the obligation that we, it must not undermine existing instruments, frameworks, and bodies. Um, so that means that where there is an existing organization such as a fisheries body, um, that is an operation that the ability of the BB and j COP to influence or to directly, um, change how that's operating is very limited.

However, um, the organization is going to be expected, the cop, the, um, what is expected to create mechanisms for coordination and cooperation so that there is more, uh, synergy between those organizations. Um, and individual states are, of course, welcome to take the, um, the objectives and measures that are recommended by the COP through to those individual organizations.

So overall, it's quite possible that this agreement will lift the performance of some of those other organizations. Other questions were asked about accountability under the agreement. Um, so first, all parties will have, uh, obligations to report on certain matters, and that always improves accountability and transparency, which can lead to international pressure if things are not going well.

Um, there is also a dispute settlement body that will, um, entail binding decisions if there are concerns about how the states are implementing it. And there is also an implementation and compliance committee, which takes a facilitative and non serial approach to improving compliance. So overall in relation to other a law of the sea bodies, this agreement has a reasonable amount of accountability.

So actually I'll pause here and invite any questions that anyone might have. Excellent. All right. Thank you very much for that summary. Uh, appreciate that. And I also just wanted to, uh, thank you for your comments in relation to the government officials. Uh, it's always easy to hear negative feedback. We don't always hear positive feedback, so I'm sure appreciate That.

Know they do work very hard on behalf of us. They do. They'll Probably replay this now. Yeah. Tim Costley. Um, thank you. I think through the submissions we've heard, I've got really, particularly from people like yourself that have a, a, you know, a really solid history with this. We good overall understanding, but what I've struggled to get some really tangible examples of what comes from this.

Mm-hmm. I understand it sets up the framework and the Yep. Pools will sort of follow. But do you have examples, for example, uh, on the, you know, promoting cooperation on marine research and sharing new technology, or do you have some other specific examples that will give us something tangible Yeah. Latch on. Um, well, just relating to marine genetic resources, of course, um, there's going to be a lot more information available to the International Scientific Committee because when, um, scientists are going out and doing research on the high seas, they're going

to be expected to provide a lot of information about not only what they're doing and what they're collecting, but also about the outcome and the products that are, that result from that. And of course, that's something that could be used by New Zealand scientists, um, in developing, its doing their own research in relation to the area based management tools.

I included in my submission an example where, let's imagine that something's happening on, in the, um, Tasman Sea and it might, a new activity has risen, which might have an impact on fisheries interests, for example. So one thing that New Zealand might be interested in doing would be to look to create an area based management tool or marine protected area, which might limit or indeed prohibit that new activity that would otherwise have an impact on fisheries.

Um, and also it gives New Zealand more information about what's happening on the high seas, including areas close to New Zealand and gives New Zealand the ability to comment on, uh, proposals by other countries to do things that might have an impact on us. And, and as I understand it, the tools, although they can happen geographically, and there's a focus in the, in the, um, agreement on sort of regional approaches, the tools once they're agreed on actually applied globally.

That's right. So How, how does that work in the context of your Tasman example? Yes, we agree on this tool to sort of manage this unforeseen impact on fisheries. Mm-hmm. Does that now apply in the North Atlantic as well? No. Well, so what happens is states can c come with proposals to create an area based management tool or marine protected area.

Um, and if that is adopted by the conference of parties, then it is binding on all states who are party to the, the, um, the treaty unless they expressly opt out. And there is an ability, if, for example, states feel they've been railroaded by other states and there's a majority vote they can opt out.

So this is an important pressure valve that was insisted on by states when, um, the agreement was being negotiated. But yes, um, the ability for it to apply to all states that party to the agreement is a real, is real benefit, But it only applies in that area. And it only applies in that area. Applies. That's right. Yeah.

And, and, and if I can just, just one, one last one. The, the pressure valve. I hadn't heard that before mm-hmm. Maybe I've just missed it, apologize. Mm-hmm. But does, does that weaken this? Yep. So there was a long debate about decision making and the COP for proposals to create marine protected areas. And a few countries really were pushing for consensus, which means that all countries would have to agree in order for the measure to be able to be created.

However, um, that raised concerns that one or two countries could stymie the whole process and just resist, resist, resist. And, and whereas the entire community might want to create it, one or two countries might stymie it. So they then decided on a decision making process. Um, and the decision making will allow a majority vote.

Now, um, because of sovereignty, states are very reluctant to sign up to agreement where they might have to implement measures that they don't agree with. Hmm. So it is common in international agreements for the ability to opt out in this agreement. It's a much more constrained process than other agreements. So there are limitations on the ability to opt out.

It has to be for particular reasons, and states are expected to impose sort of similar measures to achieve the outcome. So there, it's a much more restricted opt out than you find in other agreements. So yes, the answer is, um, it might, we might see one or two states, you know, opting out, um, in relation to A MPA or A BMT, but, um, that that is a necessary inclusion in the agreement to ensure that other states will be comfortable in signing up to the agreement. Thank You. That's really Tiana to on, uh, Thank you.

Thank you very much for your submission. I, I was just reading through the conclusion and, and I wonder if you could share with us the importance of getting in there early. Yeah, I think he, he, you say, you talk about, well, if we ratify the, we should ratify this, and when we do that, we should get in there as early as possible to how set the direction. Yes. So I wonder if you could share with, uh, members the importance of being at that scene setting stage.

Right. Um, well last time, um, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs said that they were thinking that 2026 would be the D date When this treaty entered into effect at the first meeting of the conference of parties, a lot of very important decisions are going to be made about the makeup of the institutions, the budgets, a whole range of different things that are actually quite important, I think, to how it operates.

In my view. It it, while New Zealand can be involved in the preparatory commission that's currently trying to work on some of that stuff, New Zealand wouldn't be able to vote on any of those things if it wasn't a party already, uh, at the time that comes into effect. So my recommendation is that New Zealand aims to be part of that initial, um, group of countries so that when the treaty comes into effect, we can have a say in how it operates.

Yeah. And do you have a sense of who's already committed to getting in there quickly? Um, well, I know Australia, well, I've heard, I don't know, but I've heard that Australia is aiming to ratify, um, the agreement in time for the, our Oceans Conference in the middle of this year. Um, certainly two EU states have already ratified France and Spain.

Um, and I'm, I'm confident that there are moves in other places too, to also ratify, but at the moment, the majority of the states that have, um, ratified have been developing countries. So I think it's important for New Zealand to, to, to be involved, you know, at the beginning. Yeah. Very good. Uh, final question on, thank you, just a, a bit of an opportunistic one.

Um, do you think that this agreement might have any influence over the recent announcement around the cooks and deep sea mining, the effects of that? Well, I understand that the intentions of the Cook Islands is to undertake deep sea bed mining in the National Maritime Zone, so it wouldn't have any impact, uh, on that.

I guess it's a flow on. Yeah, Yeah. No, I understand. Absolutely. Very good. Alright, well thank you very much for your time this morning. Appreciate the insights. Thank you. Thank for your work. Okay, thank you. You come to our next submitter, the High Seas Alliance via Zoom. Yes. Great. Okay.

Right, I'm here. Good morning Duncan members. Can you Yes, good morning. I can hear you fine. Thank you. You hear us? I'm actually in, actually in, in Chile, but I think my connection's not too bad. Um, oh, well Done. Excellent. Well, it looks nice over there. That's beautiful. I'm glad to say here. I'm here for the South Pacific meeting. Wonderful.

Just a brief introduction to myself. I'm an international lawyer. Um, I'm act as international legal advisor to the Diocese Alliance. I'm also New Zealand Barrister resident in Christchurch. I've attended pretty much every bb and j negotiations. It's, or around 2008. Um, a large part of my current activities is to give presentations on different aspects of the bb and j agreement to countries, um, what we call capacity building, um, most recently to, uh, to Val and to the Marshall Islands.

Um, and I'm on the way to give a presentation actually to Bhutan, believe it or not, um, as well as to prepare for the PrepCom. Um, I, I saw, uh, the professor's presentation, and I, I agree with it. I also won't go through my, my presentation, my, uh, submission. I'm sure you've read it. Um, uh, as Joanna said, there are currently, I think 17 rev ratifications lately, France and Spain, which is very exciting because these are two very significant, large developed countries.

So, um, it does, as Joanna said, turn around the, um, the appearance that there's a lot of only developing states arrive, uh, there. And we expect o other European countries to join soon as well as, as well. I think the Marshall Islands have signaled they'll be joining in in the next, uh, couple of weeks.

I understand. And just briefly about the heis Alliance, just to remind you, um, I'm sure you've read our presentation that we're a coalition of about 40 non-government organizations concerned with the conservation of the high seas. Um, we've been very much at the forefront of advocating for the BB and j treaty since, um, the negotiations started really, uh, we have worked closely with the New Zealand mission and New York and, and organizing again, what we call capacity building activities and, and, and involving delegates, um, uh, getting them up

to speed on the bb and j agreement. Uh, we've looked an active role in the negotiation of the bb and j agreement, um, and, um, and both in the wording and in encouraging countries to come along and be part of it. And, uh, yeah, we, our, our current focus is ramification primarily as well as preparing for the PrepCom, which starts on, uh, I think the 13th of April in New York.

Uh, that, that's the preparatory commission, um, which will be developing things like the rules of procedure modalities, terms of reference for the, for the, both the conference of the parties and the various subsidiary bodies, dispute resolution, uh, those sorts of things. So that's quite an important, um, meeting. In fact, there's a meeting in, in New York in the last week of, of, uh, this month, um, to prepare for that.

So I think that's probably all I have to say on that, and I'm very happy to take any questions, uh, from the committee. Excellent. Thank you very much for that summary. Uh, we have read your submission, um, appreciate the consistent vibe. We're picking up from a number of these submissions around the willingness to see, uh, prioritization on the ratification process.

Uh, and also just picking up on your comments around the consensus, uh, striving for consensus piece rather than, uh, unanimity being required. And, and we just heard that from Joanna as well. I think it's one of the areas where we have seen challenges and, and other agreements such as the Antarctic treaty system, which requires consensus, can, you know, of course it's great to have that consensus, but it does open up for some states to have a different view and to be able to hold up progress.

So I think yeah, highlighting that again as shows we're in a good place on this one. Um, but anything questions from members? Excellent. Alright. Enjoy your time and Chile and Bhutan Too easy. Thank you members, and I look forward to your deliberations.
Thank you very much. Appreciate it. Bye-bye. Okay, and we also, via Zoom, we have Greenpeace.

Goda. Good morning. Good morning, Goda. Hi. Hello. Welcome. We've, we've seen your submission. Hopefully you've been following, uh, the previous submitters in this morning's session. Uh, we have 10 minutes set down for your submission, uh, to, for you to speak to your submission today, uh, including questions over to you. Thank you. Yes, yes, I have.

And yeah, I, uh, uh, first of all, I would like to thank the committee for the opportunity to appear today and express a green peace, a strong support for the early ratification and implementation of the BB NJ Agreement by New Zealand, also known as the Global Ocean Treaty. My name is Juan Perra. I am the ocean's campaigner for Greenpeace, and Greenpeace is also a member of the High Seas Alliance.

So we are very much in support of this ratification. Um, I would like to acknowledge, first of all, the process done by signing the treaty and thus signaling New Zealand's intention to further multilateral oceanic protection and the stewardship of the living oceans. For the benefit of everyone on this planet, um, I would like to speak on behalf of tens of thousands of New Zealanders who have raised their voices in support of protecting the oceans.

Um, speak for the generations yet to come, who deserve to inherit a world where the oceans are teaming with life and not choking on pollution or strip bare by exploitation. A green pea, uh, urges the New Zealand parliament to ratify the VBNJ agreement without delay, because more than just a treaty, this is really a beacon, one of hope for the help of our planet and a testament to the power of collective action and multilateralism among nations.

So I will focus on the meaningful and widespread support for disagreement from the general public in out there and New Zealand, and the ways it will help communities here and around the Pacific facing the challenges of, uh, changing climate and a biodiversity crisis. So, um, over the decades, Greenpeace has already worked alongside other communities, advocates and leaders across the globe to push for disagreement here in Ottawa, that groundswell support is undeniable.

In February, 2022, over 60,000 New Zealanders had signed a petition calling for a strong global ocean treaty. And since, since then, uh, public support has ground louder. Recent polling from last year, December last year shows that, uh, 72% of New Zealanders want stronger laws to protect the oceans, and 79% wants better protection for the high seas right here in the South Pacific.

This is clearly an overwhelming majority calling for bold action. Um, you would agree that is not very often that New Zealanders so broadly agree on that issue in this way, but when it comes to the oceans, it seems that everyone agrees that we must manage them better. So although these numbers are significant and growing, it's more important to remember that they represent a cross-section of society that are united in their view, that we cannot have a healthy planet without healthy oceans.

They are the Maki who marvel at the beauty of marine life. They are the families who know that our wellbeing is intimately tied to the health of the oceans. They're the fishers who depend on healthy oceans for life, for their livelihoods. And they are migrants like me from all over the world who are arrived here crossing those very waters some recently and some generations ago.

They are also the scientists that understand that a healthy ocean play a crucial role in absorbing carbon and mitigating the worst effects of climate change. Uh, New Zealand is a con, is the one, is the country with one of the largest, uh, exclusive economic zones in the world. So we do have a larger responsibility than most to make sure that we're looking after the waters that surround us and that are so deeply steep in history and part of our national identity.

So for this, the bb and j agreement gives us a once in a generation chance to do better for the ocean, because it establishes the mechanisms to create marine protected areas. It ensures fair sharing of marine resources, and it requires environmental impact assessments for activities that could harm our oceans. So that said, it's an essential tool to achieve in global biodiversity targets as well.

Um, ratifying disagreement, it's not only the right thing to do, it is also what the people of Outra want, as I've said before. And by acting now, we would show the world that New Zealand is a nation that listens to its people that values the oceans. Its international partnerships and that it's prepared to lead. Uh, we like to think of sales as a good Pacific neighbors, and that means acting on the benefit of the ocean and of the nations that depend on the ocean for their survival.

So we of course, endorse the submission of the Heiss Alliance, which highlights the technical and legal importance of early ratification. It is, uh, as this has mentioned, has been mentioned before, known that 60 countries need to ratify the treaty before it comes into effect. And the more nations that do it, the more legitimacy in this international instrument will have.

And New Zealand has the chance to be one of the early movers and have more influence in where it it will go. UMO has this chance to lead, to stand in solidarity with our Pacific neighbors who are already disproportionately affected by climate change and the consequences of reckless exploitation of the oceans. So the message that, uh, we bring today is simple.

New Zealand has the mandate, the opportunity, and the responsibility to act. Now. We are very nearly there. We have taken the first steps. So let's finish the job and be proud of the part we played by ratifying this agreement. We will send the message that our oceans are not just commodities to be exploited, they are treasures to be protected.

So the people of the OFA and the communities of the Pacifics are watching us. They have spoken out on this issue before because they know how much is at stake. Together we can ensure that this agreement marks a turning point for our oceans. So let us seize this moment and lead with courage for the oceans, for the future, and for the people.

That brings us to an end for the public submissions on the BB and j bill today. Okay. Thank, uh, we'll move straight on to public submissions for the agreement on climate change, trade and sustain ability. There are touch ahead of time. Do we have Professor Kelsey online? Excellent. Great.

All right. Good morning, professor Kelsey, wonderful to see you again, and thank you for your time submitting on the agreement on climate change, trade and sustainability, uh, amongst others. But the topic of today's conversation, uh, we have 10 minutes set down for your submission today. We have received the written version of that.

Um, we welcome your comments speaking to it. Thank you, Gilda. Thank you, chair. Um, this submission is, uh, quite short because, uh, there's not actually a lot to say about this agreement. Um, I, I note though that I have a submission on the U-A-E-F-T-A, which was much more detailed, and I would request a longer period of time to address the complexities in that.

Uh, in relation to the A-C-C-T-S. Um, it has of course been negotiated, um, behind closed doors. There's been very little information available. However, I have monitored it actively, uh, and, uh, made several presentations on it in Geneva at the WTO Public Forum.

Um, I've also, um, been, uh, an advisor in the context of Naga, um, but am not speaking on their behalf, uh, or using any of the information secured from that. I gather, uh, Maui Solomon will be, uh, presenting that submission shortly. Um, put succinctly, the A-C-C-T-S is another example of what we are seeing increasingly, uh, through trade agreements of greenwashing in the name of, um, addressing climate change.

So the four pillars, as, as you're aware, um, the first two of them are effectively to advance the liberalization of goods and services that has been stalled at the WTO for a number of years. And again, that is something that I've followed quite closely. Um, it, it was interesting to see the minister and, uh, van Gall vital when he was talking to you, effectively conceding that it's basically a commercial agreement.

They focused on what the commercial gains to New Zealand, uh, would be, uh, from the liberalization, especially, uh, of so-called environmental goods. Uh, although the lists of both goods and services, uh, is extremely extensive, um, it has nothing really to do with addressing the climate crisis.

Uh, indeed, one has to even wonder when we are dealing with the commercial gains, when we look at Iceland, Switzerland, Costa Rica, and New Zealand, uh, just what the commercial gains there will be. And even if, uh, Mr. Vital is correct that others might sign onto this, it is not gonna make the slightest bit of difference to the climate crisis.

And I'm happy to provide more, uh, critique of why Liberalizing Environmental Goods and services does not do that. Uh, if you would, like, I just note, for example, one instance in relation to services, which is a area of my specialization, I can't see for the life of me, how liberalizing foreign direct investment, which is mode three on wastewater, sewage, waste disposal, et cetera, in private companies, contracting other companies, is going to advance climate strategies.

Um, indeed, it's more likely to risk posing barriers because it restricts the ability to regulate, uh, the environmental activities of those companies and the ones that they contract. Um, pillar four, voluntary eco labeling is neither here nor there.

It's not gonna change the world. Um, I want to focus, uh, for the rest of my submission on, uh, pillar three harmful fossil fuel subsidies, which in theory would have the potential to make some difference. Um, however, what we've seen in the, uh, in the negotiations, uh, and in the final text is that Norway has consistently negotiated down, uh, what is under that pillar, and then decided not to sign.

And I understand has no intention of signing. Uh, you can, if you look at the text, actually run a bus through the definitions of harmful fossil fuel subsidies, the exceptions, the schedules, the complex criteria and mechanisms to be essentially meaningless.

And if, if Mr. Vital sees this as a precedent to be set internationally, then it ain't gonna get us nowhere. Um, however, since I made the submission, I've read a paper by Rory McLeod, who's a former MFA official, a pro market competition expert, uh, a paper that he wrote for Peck, uh, for APEC on trade and climate change in June last year.

And he identified four areas where trade and climate change would genuinely contribute to significant, um, impacts on the climate crisis. The four are dealing with carbon leakage, dealing with tariff and non tariff barrier escalation, thirdly, environmentally harmful subsidies, and fourthly, compatibility of trade rules with climate change policies or the incompatibility.

So let me just focus on the third of these very briefly, because what Rory McLeod highlights is the need to deal with explicit and implicit subsidies where emitters are not taxed or charged for the real costs of their emissions. Implicit subsidies includes subsidies for methane from agriculture, environmentally damaging behavior, not having to pay for it is a subsidy not covered in any way by, uh, pillar three of this agreement.

Just to put it in context, the World Bank has estimated that total greenhouse gases from agriculture account for one quarter of the world's total greenhouse gas emissions, almost all untaxed and unregulated subsidies. Governments at notes are unwilling to discipline them, but if we included New Zealand, Australia, and Chile's implicit subsidies, we would look very different.

And hence, we can see why they're not in this agreement. Uh, Rory McLeod cites a study by Mike Joy and others that implicit subsidies for meat and dairy production in alt amount to, uh, just under $1 billion. So if we are serious about addressing climate change in the trade context, we need to address these issues, not the kind of greenwashing we see in the A-C-C-T-S.

His final point, which is in relation to the clash between, uh, in climate change policies and trade agreements, highlights something that I talked to you about in terms of the anta, which is investor state dispute settlement, where companies are bringing investment disputes to challenge, uh, four billions of dollars climate change policies, and to use that as a chilling effect to deter governments from implementing them.

So I would urge the committee to, um, take seriously the inadequacies of the A-C-C-T-S and recommend more significant action is taken to address the trade and climate issues. I'm happy to provide both Rory McLeod's paper and Mike Joy and others paper, if that will be helpful.

All right. Thank you very much. Uh, first question, Tim Costley. Thanks Mr. Chair. Um, thanks for that, Jane, nice to see you again. I'm a little bit, I don't know if sad is the word, but it, it feels like, you know, even in your written submission you said that this will, you know, it does make it difference. You called it minimal, but it's not harming our, our climate.

Every, I I thought we were at the point where every little bit counts and, and, and, and other submitters have said, let's just encourage others to get on board with this. Um, why would we be wanting to run away from this if, if it even makes a little bit of difference? Because if we take that line, critics will say, well, why would New Zealand bother with climate change at all?

'cause we're so small. So what would your response to them be? My response is very simple. Climate change is urgent. Uh, we need to address the things that will make a real difference, not hypothetically, might make a marginal difference. And, and the marginality, believe me, is extremely marginal. But Isn't anything better than nothing?

No, because what it does is act as a diversion. You look at the amount of resources that MFA has poured into this when it could have been doing something substantial. They've now got at least three other examples where negotiations are going down a similar track. If we put those resources into something that was genuinely meaningful, then I would be fully supportive of it.

And that's one of the points that Rory McLeod is making in, in his, uh, in his paper for Peck, that APEC dropped the ball on the climate issues. It's now acknowledged it's a priority, but it needs to address these real issues. So I understand that. But now that they've done the work, if it makes even a bit of difference, why wouldn't we just take it and, and go, sure, next time you want to see more, but why wouldn't we Now that all that work's been invested, and, and I appreciate your view that that wasn't well spent,

but why wouldn't we just just at least do this as a first Step? Because what it does is signal that that's okay. And, and I can tell you, I, as I said, I'm very happy to provide you with, um, analysis of why, for example, trade in services liberalization and trade in goods liberalization, uh, would have at best and extremely minimal impact, because there is no clear link between the liberalization, for example, of trade and services and addressing the climate crisis.

And I don't know whether the people, other people who have, uh, addressed the submission actually have the expertise that I do on trade and services. I've worked on it since 1990 intensively internationally. And I can tell you that there are more barriers that these commitments were put in place than there are advantages in being able to address the climate crisis.

And if I thought the committee was seriously going to analyze the agreement, I would have provided you with those details. But my experience to date would suggest that that's a waste of time. Final question from the honorable Damien O'Connor. Uh, look, thank you, Jane.

Um, how much influence do you think we have in the world of international trade? It depends on what it is. Um, as, as, as you know, as a, as a former minister, mean, It depends on what it's, yes. Well, It depends on what the issues are, uh, and where New Zealand's, um, commercial interest lie.

And, and as you know, New Zealand, uh, advances agreements that it sees predominantly trade agreements as it sees predominantly in New Zealand's commercial interests. And and I mentioned that New Zealand as, as you again, will know, has been a major advocate along with Costa Rica and Norway and Switzerland for the liberalization of environmental goods and services, not for climate change reasons, but for commercial reasons. And So, well, how do you think, how do you think that the world is going to address these issues if we don't share the best knowledge

services and technology and do it in a way that is not interrupted by non tariff barriers or direct intervention? If you were going to do that, you would address it, for example, by changing the agreement on trade related investment measures that allow for technology transfer, you would deal with it in the digital trade. Do, Do you, I I mean, I think, Sorry, let, lemme let me finish my answer.
